items: Book the venue and caterer first, they are the most in-demand vendors.
In peak season, a venue is often booked 12 to 18 months ahead.
Set a date or date range before contacting vendors.
Wedding planning goes smoother with one centralized, regularly updated task list.
Paperwork (marriage license, town hall) often takes longer than expected.
Have a backup plan for rain if your ceremony or reception is outdoors.
A month-by-month timeline stops tasks from piling up in the final weeks.
Quote requests take time, follow up with a vendor if you haven't heard back in a week.
Keep a signed copy of every contract, ideally all in one place.
The final stretch (30 days out) goes smoother with a dedicated day-of checklist.
A wedding planner isn't required, but having one point person on the day changes everything.
Leave a good buffer between key moments to absorb any delays.
Saturday weddings often cost more than weekday or Sunday ones.
Marrying off-season (fall, winter) often means better rates.
A weekday wedding can meaningfully cut your venue and catering bill.
Keep a written record of every important vendor exchange.
Consider cancellation insurance if you're investing heavily well ahead of time.
The day goes fast, keep ten minutes just for the two of you in the schedule.
Notify the town hall early if you're changing your name to avoid last-minute delays.
A hair and makeup trial before the day avoids unpleasant surprises.
Pack an emergency kit for the day: pins, thread, tissues, stain remover.
Assign one person to handle gifts and cash at the end of the night.
A shared run-of-show with key vendors avoids mix-ups on the day.
Confirm exact timings with every vendor about a week before the wedding.
Have a backup ride ready in case your wedding car runs into trouble.
In the month before the wedding, prioritize the irreversible tasks first (invitations, contracts).
A wedding at home or at a relative's place often needs more logistics than it looks.
Leave extra time for getting ready in the morning, everything takes longer than planned.
A checklist per category (venue, budget, guests, vendors) helps you not miss the essentials.
Vendor briefs work better written down, not just said out loud.
Have an on-site emergency contact who isn't the bride or groom.
The first six months of planning are mostly about locking date, venue, and budget.
Weddings typically take 12 to 18 months to plan, but less time is possible too.
Keep every vendor's contact info in one document everyone can access.
Have a backup playlist ready in case the DJ hits a technical issue.
Always add a 5 to 10% buffer to your budget for surprises.
Venue and catering often make up half of a wedding's total budget.
Always ask for an itemized quote rather than a vague flat package.
Compare at least three quotes before signing with a vendor.
Deposits are often non-refundable, read the cancellation terms before signing.
Tracking spending by category helps catch overruns before they add up.
Per-head catering costs vary a lot depending on the service style you choose.
Budget a line for tips and service charges, it's easy to forget.
Delivery and setup fees aren't always included in a florist's quote.
Negotiating is easier off-season or on a weekday than a Saturday in June.
A per-guest budget makes it easier to match guest count to what you can spend.
Set aside a budget line for outfits, rings included.
Photography and video usually take up 10 to 15% of a wedding budget.
Some vendors charge a different rate on Sundays or public holidays.
Keep a reserve for thank-you gifts to your witnesses and close helpers.
A clear contribution table avoids misunderstandings when families help fund the budget.
Budget for travel and lodging if your vendors are coming from far away.
Last-minute costs (alterations, extras) are easier to absorb with a buffer built in.
Send save-the-dates at least 6 months ahead for peak-season weddings.
Formal invitations usually go out 2 to 3 months before the wedding.
Set an RSVP deadline at least 3 weeks before the event.
Follow up individually with guests who haven't responded after the deadline.
Average no-show rate is around 10%, worth keeping in mind for the caterer's count.
Collect dietary needs and allergies right at RSVP, not at the last minute.
Grouping your guest list by circle (close family, friends, coworkers) helps you prioritize on a tight budget.
Decide your policy on kids (invited or not) before invitations go out.
Seating charts come together more easily once every RSVP is in.
Let out-of-town guests know early so they can book lodging in time.
An online RSVP page is easier to track than paper reply cards.
Make it clear on each invitation whether a "plus one" is included.
Guests rarely mention a change in dietary needs unless you ask directly.
Personal thank-you notes go faster if you log gifts as they arrive.
Keep a backup guest list in case of late cancellations.
Split the ceremony guest list from the reception one if space is limited.
A shared address list makes sending invitations and thank-yous much easier.
Leave more time than you think for group photos with a large guest list.
Share the dress code early if you have one, so guests have time to plan.
A shuttle for guests avoids parking headaches at out-of-the-way venues.
Meet a vendor before signing, a video call or in person avoids bad surprises.
Check a vendor's recent reviews, not just their overall rating.
Always ask what's included in a package before comparing prices.
A vendor contract should spell out timing, location, and cancellation terms.
Photographers and venues are usually the first to get booked, ahead of everyone else.
Ask to see a full wedding album, not just a highlight reel.
Give your DJ or band a do-not-play list, not just a wish list.
Schedule a coordination meeting with key vendors in the month before the wedding.
Check a quote's expiry date before letting it lapse.
Caterers often offer a tasting before you sign, don't hesitate to ask for one.
Get any last-minute change with a vendor confirmed in writing.
Florists work best with specific inspiration photos rather than a vague description.
A booking deposit is often 20 to 30% of the total.
Check that a vendor carries professional liability insurance.
Factor in travel costs for a photographer or videographer coming from far away.
A vague quote that pushes you to sign fast deserves extra thought.
Ask vendors for a backup plan in case of bad weather for an outdoor wedding.
Start your seating chart with the head table, everything else builds around it.
Avoid mixing two groups who don't know each other without at least one familiar face at the table.
Set up a kids' table if several families have children, with one adult nearby.
Finalize the seating chart in the final week, once RSVPs are all in.
Always leave one or two spare seats per table for last-minute surprises.
Seat families with known tension apart rather than hoping for the best.
A round table of 8 to 10 encourages more conversation than one of 12.
Post the seating chart at the entrance to help guests find their seats.
Use legible place cards, especially for less common names.
A digital seating chart makes last-minute changes easier than paper ever could.
A clear mood board helps vendors understand your style at a glance.
Stick to 2 or 3 main colors for a cohesive decor.
In-season flowers are often cheaper and hold up better on the day.
A theme doesn't need to show up everywhere, a few touches are enough.
Use warm lighting for the evening, it completely changes how photos feel.
Decor that works with the venue usually takes less effort than building a look from scratch.
A creative guestbook (polaroids, time capsule) leaves a livelier trace than a plain notebook.
Test your flower choices in person before the day, colors read differently on screen.
A simple photobooth corner is often what guests remember longest.
A personal touch (an object, a story, a family tradition) sticks with guests more than a flawless decor.
